articles.title: [Comment] Intersecting vulnerabilities and homelessness in young people
articles.summary: Homelessness is a growing global problem. In 2024, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and the EU estimated that more than two million people were experiencing homelessness across the 38 member countries.1 Notably, youths consistently make up a large share. OECD and EU reports indicate that youths account for 35% of people experiencing homelessness in the Netherlands, 31% in Australia, and 28% in Denmark.2 In North America, Canada reports that 18% of people experiencing homelessness are younger than 24 years,3 and the USA reports 27%, with a 10% increase between 2023 and 2024.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Paralysis in public health: evidence as an alibi? – Authors' reply
articles.summary: We welcome the thoughtful responses to our Viewpoint1 from Michael Glick, Arnaud Chiolero and colleagues, and Nicolas Dritsch and colleagues. Together, the letters extend the discussion around analysis paralysis in public health across three related domains: evidence infrastructure, learning systems, and systems theory.

articles.title: Bristow Group To Acquire Berry Aviation For $105 Million
articles.summary: Bristow Group announced its definitive agreement to acquire Berry Aviation for $105 million in an all-cash transaction. This acquisition will expand Bristow’s Government Services platform by adding special mission aviation capabilities and long-standing relationships with U.S. defense and government customers. The deal is expected to enhance Bristow's earnings quality by increasing exposure to contracted government services and multi-mission aviation activities, and is projected to close in Q3 2026.

Ask HN: How do you test AI-generated code?
articles.summary:
When AI generates code, I first instruct the model to find, fix, and verify any issues. After that, I start the server and test whether it actually works from the user’s perspective.

What I’m looking for is a workflow where issues are received, fixed, tested, and deployed—but it seems that current AI agents aren’t very good at performing browser tests from the user’s perspective.

I’ve tried using the built-in browsers in Codex and Cursor, but they often only checked whether the page loaded. In the end, I had to instruct them step by step on what to do, and it turned out to be cheaper and faster for me to test it myself.

So I’m curious to know how you’ve set up test automation. Are there any services that do this (for individuals, not just enterprises)? If you’re using a harness like Codex, I’d like to know what instructions and skills are needed to get it to perform tests from the user’s perspective.

articles.title: Senate Votes to Limit Trump's Iran War Powers | Balance of Power: Late Edition 06/23/2026
articles.summary: "Balance of Power: Late Edition" focuses on the intersection of politics and global business. On today's show, Democratic Senator Angela Alsobrooks of Maryland says the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act shows Congress can still work in a bipartisan fashion to tackle affordability, arguing there are "many more opportunities" for lawmakers to do so. Republican Senator Roger Marshall of Kansas says the SAVE America Act is the one bill he would choose to pass if he could pass only one more in his Senate career, as President Trump presses Congress to advance the measure. Democratic Representative Sydney Kamlager-Dove of California says she is not prepared to support additional Pentagon funding without more answers from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Iran and depleted munitions stockpiles. (Source: Bloomberg)

articles.title: Sen. Alsobrooks Sees More Room for Bipartisan Deals

She also discusses the CLARITY Act and says crypto market structure legislation must include ethics protections around the President's family and other elected officials, as well as illicit finance provisions. articles.title: Ex-AT&T lawyer who raised compliance concerns accused of ethics violations
articles.summary: A former senior AT&T lawyer, Theodore Marcus, who claimed to have raised concerns about the company's compliance with a school discount program, is being accused of ethics violations. The District of Columbia Office of Disciplinary Counsel alleges Marcus disclosed confidential information to a whistleblower suing AT&T. Marcus, who resigned from AT&T in 2011, is accused of intentionally prejudicing AT&T and violating attorney conflict-of-interest rules.
